title: Listening - Part 4: Guiding Marine Wildlife Trips
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Ben, who guides marine wildlife boat tours. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: This morning I’m with Ben Carter, who guides visitors on boat trips to see marine wildlife. Ben, did you grow up by the sea? || Ben: No, I lived in a busy town several hours inland. I loved documentaries about whales, but I never thought they could lead to employment. [24]During a family holiday I joined a wildlife boat trip, and watching the guide explain what we were seeing made me realise it was a possible job[/24]. I started volunteering on trips while studying. || Presenter: What do visitors usually expect from a tour? || Ben: Most hope for dolphins leaping beside the boat, and sometimes we are lucky. But these are wild animals, not performers. [25]At the beginning of every trip, I explain that we cannot guarantee a sighting and that enjoying the sea itself is part of the experience[/25]. People cope much better with disappointment when expectations are honest. || Presenter: How do you find animals out at sea? || Ben: We study weather, tide and recent reports from other boats, but simply looking carefully is still essential. Birds can be particularly helpful. [26]When seabirds gather and repeatedly dive in one area, it often indicates fish are present, and larger animals may be feeding nearby[/26]. So I tell passengers to watch the sky as well as the water. || Presenter: Does taking tourists out ever harm wildlife? || Ben: It can if boats behave badly. A dolphin needs space to choose where it goes. Our company follows a strict code: [27]we slow down at a distance and never chase an animal or position the boat between members of a group[/27]. Seeing wildlife is exciting, but its welfare has priority. || Presenter: Is the work enjoyable in poor weather? || Ben: It’s definitely less comfortable. Wind makes communication difficult and some passengers feel ill. The greatest challenge, though, is making a decision before leaving. [28]There are days when I must cancel a tour even though passengers are keen to go, because conditions would not be safe[/28]. Nobody likes that conversation. || Presenter: What skills did you have to develop? || Ben: I needed knowledge of animals, of course, and safety training. But I used to give very technical explanations whenever something appeared. [29]I learned that visitors remember much more when I describe an animal clearly and allow them time simply to observe it[/29]. Information should improve the moment, not interrupt it. || Presenter: What would you recommend to someone interested in your job? || Ben: Spend time at sea before paying for courses. Some people love marine wildlife but discover that boats make them miserable. [30]Volunteering with a responsible tour operator is the best way to find out whether the lifestyle actually suits you[/30]. You also learn how much work happens when no animals appear.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What made Ben consider working as a wildlife guide?

options.A: A guide he observed during a holiday trip.
options.B: A documentary about whales he saw as a child.
options.C: A volunteer position offered during his studies.

questions.explanation: Ben realised guiding was a possible job after watching a guide explain sightings on a holiday boat trip.

questions.question: What does Ben tell passengers before each trip?

options.A: Dolphins are easier to see in certain seasons.
options.B: The trip may be worthwhile even without sightings.
options.C: Visitors should not expect to take photographs.

questions.explanation: He says sightings cannot be guaranteed and enjoying the sea is part of the experience.

questions.question: How can seabirds help Ben locate marine wildlife?

options.A: Their movement can suggest where food is available.
options.B: Their calls change when larger animals arrive.
options.C: They fly back towards boats that saw animals earlier.

questions.explanation: Diving seabirds often indicate fish, which may attract larger marine animals.

questions.question: How does Ben's company protect animals?

options.A: It limits how many passengers can look at them.
options.B: It observes them without following or separating them.
options.C: It only approaches animals that are already feeding.

questions.explanation: The boats slow down, do not chase animals and do not come between members of a group.

questions.question: What is difficult about bad weather for Ben?

options.A: He may need to refuse passengers a trip for safety reasons.
options.B: He cannot tell whether wildlife is close to the boat.
options.C: He has to explain sea sickness to first-time visitors.

questions.explanation: He sometimes cancels trips despite passengers wishing to go because conditions are unsafe.

questions.question: What has Ben learned about communicating with visitors?

options.B: He should speak only after animals disappear.
options.C: Simple explanations and observation work best.

questions.explanation: He found visitors remember more when explanations are clear and they have time to observe.

questions.question: What does Ben advise potential wildlife guides to do?

options.A: Obtain safety qualifications before going on trips.
options.B: Gain experience with a good operator before committing.
options.C: Study marine animals before applying as volunteers.

questions.explanation: Ben recommends volunteering first to learn whether life at sea suits them.
